entry.title: An Introduction to Complex Fluid Dynamics and PDEs

link.title: An Introduction to Complex Fluid Dynamics and PDEs

entry.summary: an exploration into stoke's theorem for vector fields, complex analysis and partial differential equations

entry.title: Chow’s Theorem
entry.content:
I learned about how to construct Riemann surfaces in Complex Analysis today. We didn’t explicitly mention that the maps have to be holomorphic as we haven’t introduced that term yet, but from what I recall holomorphic means complex differentiable and that means analytic in the entire complex plane. We need it to be smooth enough to glue everything together, I’m assuming.

So anyway, back to Chow’s lemma. The last time I saw this was in a class on Schemes, and it was Valentine’s day. That’s all I remember but I wrote notes for it. You’re going to flip when you see what I wrote:

So anyway, after all of that, we’re still kind of stuck and this is where we’re going to stop for now, until I return to Algebraic Geometry.

Stay tuned!
entry.summary: an exploration into chow's theorem as written in Hartshorne's Algebraic Geometry

entry.title: Inverse Problem of Galois Theory
entry.content: There is an equivalence of categories that I want to mention. It’s the correspondence between finite field extensions and connected Riemann surfaces and covering spaces, but I swear I’m not going to tell you right now. It may take me by the end of this semester.
entry.summary: Revisiting the Inverse Problem, 8 years later

entry.title: You’re Not an Imposter
entry.content:
You know what bothered me the most about grad school? I couldn’t do it all alone. The rapid rate of answering those problems made me think that since I couldn’t think of them as quickly as everyone else that meant I would never know them. That haunted me. But I internalized that as me not being good enough. I always thought that since I learned slower that means I wasn’t learning at all.

That’s the rub about standardization, you can and will standardize, and you’ll learn the material the way all the rest of them learned: rote memorization. I was so against that, until I realized what learning this mathematical language actually is.

To study Kanji (Japanese characters), you don’t study the radicals, not at first, you just memorize the stroke order.

So when you’re solving a math problem, and you go to one of your classmates, math.stackexchange.com, chatgpt, or better yet me! to find an answer, don’t tell yourself you’re stupid. Don’t feel guilty for playing the game like everyone else. You’re not a loser. You’re winning! You are adapting to the environment that was laid out in front of you. This isn’t a single-player game. It’s survival of the fittest. Unfortunately, you can’t compete in this mind-building competition against other competitors and be full natty. You’re going to lose lest you take performance enhancers.

Asking for help is a type performance enhancer. Listen here, honey, the game is rigged. You’re no less of a mathematician because you read Einstein than Einstein is less of a physicist for consulting all the mathematicians and physicists before him. And Albert got help from a lot of people.

You have the internet at your disposal now. You have an infinite supply of information. But you’re not simply a computer, you can parse and interpret this information in ways that no one else can. That’s why you’re here.

So when you feel bad about asking for help, remember, everyone else did it too… and you’re here to win.
